content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 30, 2017

Molly S. Stump
City Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-014

Dear Ms. Stump:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmembers Tom Dubois,
Eric Filseth, Liz Kniss, Lydia Kou, Greg Scharff, and Greg Tanaka, and City Manager James Keene
(collectively the "Public Officials") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. This letter is based only on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does the General Plan Exception set forth in Regulation 18702.2(c)(2) permit the Public
Officials to take part in governmental decisions relating to the City's General Plan Update,
including, but not limited to, decisions regarding:

• Growth management policy options aimed at controlling non-residential
development.

• Housing policy options aimed at controlling non-residential development.

• Height limit policy options aimed at preserving the City's 50-foot height limit
or allowing flexibility as an incentive for good design and/or multifamily
housing.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-014
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Yes. The General Plan Exception applies to the General Plan Update decisions at issue
because the decisions meet the criteria of Regulation 18702.2(c)(2). Accordingly, we conclude that
the Act does not prohibit the Public Officials from taking part in those decisions.

FACTS

The City has been working on an update to its General Plan for many years. A citizens'
advisory committee has recently provided its recommendations for the General Plan Update to the
City Council for its consideration. As a result, the City Council will soon review the committee's
work and offer direction to staff regarding key issues that must be decided before the City Council
can consider the General Plan Update's adoption.

Among other decisions related to the General Plan Update, the City Council will consider
and decide on and between: (1) growth management policy options aimed at controlling non-
residential development; (2) housing policy options aimed at controlling non-residential
development; and (3) height limit policy options aimed at preserving the City's 50-foot height limit
or allowing flexibility as an incentive for good design and/or multifamily housing.

Stanford University owns considerable land within the City, including the land underlying
the Stanford Research Park, the Stanford Shopping Center, and the Stanford University Medical
Center.

Growth Management Policy Options

In considering growth management policy options, the City Council will decide whether the
General Plan Update should perpetuate, modify, or eliminate: (a) the City's longstanding
cumulative cap on non-residential development; (b) its interim annual limit on office/R&D
development; and (c) its cumulative cap on non-residential development in the downtown.

The Cumulative Cap on Non-Residential Development

The City's cumulative cap on non-residential development was established in 1998 with the
City's adoption of its current General Plan. The cumulative cap allowed for about 3.2 million
square feet of non-residential development in "monitored areas" of the City. The monitored areas
include the Stanford Research Park and the Stanford Shopping Center, but do not include the area
surrounding the Stanford University Medical Center (hereafter the "SUMC Vicinity").

The citizens' advisory committee has provided the City Council with a number of growth
management policy options, including maintaining and updating the cumulative cap. If updated the
draft policy options provided by the citizens' advisory committee would retain the 1.7 million
square feet remaining out of the original 3.2 million square feet, would apply the cap citywide
except for the SUMC Vicinity, and would apply the cap to certain non-residential uses rather than
all non-residential uses. The policy options would also continue to exempt public facilities, or could
broaden the list of exempt uses, for example by excluding medical offices.

File No. A-17-014
Page No. 3


As articulated in the current General Plan and in the citizens' committee growth management options, the cumulative cap would be an expression of the maximum amount of growth expected during the life of the updated General Plan, and would not serve as a limit on covered development unless and until the cumulative cap is reached.

The Interim Annual Limit on Office/R&D Development

In 2015, the City adopted an interim annual limit of 50,000 net new square feet of office/R&D development affect a subset of the City including the downtown, the California Avenue area, and the El Camino Real corridor. The interim ordinance limits the amount of new office space that can be approved each year and requires projects to compete for an allocation (based on a variety of factors such as quality and sustainability) if proposals exceed the limit. Rejected projects may be reconsidered the next year. In the fifteen years from FY 2001 to FY 2015, applications for office/R&D development exceeded 50,000 square feet five times.

The interim ordinance is due to expire in October 2017 and the growth management policy options provided by the citizens' advisory committee include a General Plan policy making that limit permanent in a broader area of the City. One of the options includes applying the limit citywide with an exemption for the SUMC Vicinity and the Stanford Research Park, and one of the options includes making the annual limit citywide with an exemption for both the SUMC Vicinity and the Stanford Research Park.

If the Stanford Research Park is excluded from the annual limit, the citizens' advisory committee suggests that the Council adopt a condition that a nascent collaboration of Research Park tenants continues to make progress on reducing commute trips by single occupant vehicles. If the Stanford Research Park is included in the annual limit, the citizens' advisory committee suggests possibly making the Research Park subject to a separate, additional allocation of 50,000 square feet, and allowing unused square footage to roll forward from year to year.

The Downtown Cumulative Cap on Non-Residential Development

The current General Plan includes a cumulative cap of 350,000 new square feet of non-residential development in the City's downtown with a 1986 baseline for the cap. At this time, there are less than 50,000 square feet remaining under the downtown cap. Some of the policy options presented by the citizens' advisory committee would perpetuate the cap's 350,000 square foot limit with modifications to focus on office/R&D uses only, rather than all non-residential uses, and some options would potentially include additional exemptions.

Housing Policy Options

The City Council will also soon consider whether to include within the General Plan Update policies supporting residential development of the El Camino frontage of the Stanford Research Park and the Stanford Shopping Center, the SUMC Vicinity, and transit-served areas such as downtown and California Avenue.

File No. A-17-014
Page No. 4


Height Limit Policy Options

In considering height-limit options, the City Council will be asked to perpetuate the City's
50' height limit as a policy in the General Plan Update, or to allow the limit to be exceeded when
necessary for good design (e.g. taller retail and residential floor-to-ceiling heights) or as an
incentive for affordable or multifamily housing. If exceedances are allowed, they would be limited
to transit-served areas, which could be interpreted to include parts of the Stanford Research Park
and the Stanford Shopping Center, as well as the downtown and California Avenue areas.

The Public Officials

The Public Officials' respective potential financial interests in the decisions at issue are as
follows:

• Councilmember Dubois's spouse is employed by Stanford University.²

• Councilmembers Kou and Tanaka each have ownership interests in their
respective primary residences, and each of those residences is within 500 feet
of the Stanford Research Park.

• Councilmember Filseth and City Manager Keene each have ownership
interests in their respective primary residences, and each of those residences is
within 500 feet of the downtown cap area.

• Councilmember Scharff has an ownership interest in commercial real property
within 500 feet of the downtown cap area. This property is zoned for multi-
family residential, with commercial uses permissible via conditional use
permit or grandfathering. The property is leased to a medical office for five
years, running to June 2018, and the lessee has an option to extend the lease
for one additional five-year period, potentially running to June 2023.

• Councilmember Kniss has an ownership interest in multi-family residential
real property within 500 feet of the California Avenue area. This property
produces rental income.

The Stanford Lands

Stanford University is organized as a trust with corporate powers and tax exempt status
under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3). Stanford has gross revenues approximately
equivalent to that of a Fortune 500 company, and owns large areas of commercial and other lands
within City limits. Stanford has not subdivided its lands into separate legal parcels pursuant to

² A suit for the dissolution of the marriage has been filed. You state that Councilmember Dubois will seek
FPPC advice regarding the continuing application of the source of income rules in light of the pending dissolution in a
separate request.

File No. A-17-014
Page No. 5

California law, but rather divides the non-academic areas of the Stanford lands into discrete "lease
lots," which the City and Santa Clara County treat as equivalent to parcels for planning purposes.

The Stanford Research Park comprises approximately 700 acres within the City. The
Research Park is divided into approximately 104 separate ground lease lots which the City
considers "parcels" for planning and entitlement purposes, and to which the County has assigned
Assessor's Parcel Numbers ("APNs"). Most of these lots are leased by private companies under
various terms. Many of the leases are long term. Ground lessees include big companies like Ford
Motor Company, Hewlett Packard, Merck, SAP, Silicon Valley Bank, Tesla, VMWare, and Wilson
Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati, as well as smaller companies and developers. Development
applications are typically submitted for a single lot.³ Typically, the lessee will initiate, finance, and
control a proposed building project, with Stanford's approval as landowner.

The SUMC Vicinity comprises 54 acres and is divided into approximately 11 separate
ground lease lots, each with its own APN. The Lucile Packard Children's Hospital, the Stanford
University Medical Center, and Stanford Hospital are the major tenants and these facilities are
currently being redeveloped consistent with a June 6, 2011 development agreement between
Stanford and the City. The balance of the area is leased to smaller medical office tenants.

The Stanford Shopping Center comprises 70 acres. It is divided into six separate lots, each
with its own APN, which are leased to and managed by Simon Properties. The City's zoning code
limits the amount of development on the site to what currently exists today. A new development
agreement would be required for additional capacity or any hotel or mixed use development.

Together, the Stanford Research Park, SUMC Vicinity, and Stanford Shopping Center
comprise 824 acres, or approximately 15 percent of the City's developed area.⁴ You state that none
of the policy options at issue pertain to a specific lot or lots, or would consider or approve any
particular development proposal.

You are the City Attorney for the City of Palo Alto and the authorized representative of the
Public Officials. You have asked whether the General Plan Exception set forth in Regulation
18702.2(c)(2) applies to the General Plan Update decisions at issue, permitting the Public Officials
to take part in those decisions under the Act.

ANALYSIS

Sections 87100 and 87103 prohibit a public official from taking part in a governmental
decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the
official's financial interests.

³ However, Stanford itself has occasionally submitted applications for larger projects, such as faculty and
student housing.

⁴ This calculation excludes the following Stanford lands: approximately 9,780 acres of undevelopable lands
such as public conservation, park, or protected open space, and approximately 396 additional acres located throughout
the City.

File No. A-17-014
Page No. 6

Each of the Public Officials has at least one financial interest identified in Section 87103
that is implicated by the General Plan Update decisions at issue:

• Councilmember Dubois has a source of income interest in Stanford University due to its
employment of his spouse.

• Councilmembers Kou, Tanaka, and Filseth and City Manager Keene have real property
interests in their respective residences, each of which is within 500 feet of either the
Stanford Research Park or the downtown cap area.

• Councilmember Scharff has both business and real property interests in his commercial real
property leased to a medical office and in close proximity to the downtown cap area.

• Councilmember Kniss has real property, business, and source of income interests in her
multi-family residential real property that produces rental income and is within 500 feet of
the California Avenue area.

Regulation 18702.2(c)(2) sets forth an exception to that prohibition for certain decisions
relating to general plans and general plan amendments, and provides as follows:

"(c) Exceptions: …

"(2) The decision solely concerns the adoption or amendment of a general plan
and all of the following apply:

"(A) The decision only identifies planning objectives or is otherwise
exclusively one of policy. A decision will not qualify under this subdivision if
the decision is initiated by the public official, by a person that is a financial
interest to the public official, or by a person representing either the public
official or a financial interest to the public official.

"(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public
official's agency before implementing the planning or policy's objectives, such
as permitting, licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or a change to a zoning
variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent.

"(C) The decision does not concern an identifiable parcel or parcels or
development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel or
parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which the
decision is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area depicted
on a map or diagram offered in connection with the decision, provided the map
or diagram depicts all parcels located within the agency's jurisdiction and
economic interests of the official are not singled out.

File No. A-17-014
Page No. 7

"(D) The decision does not concern the agency's prior, concurrent, or
subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, license, zoning designation,
zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent."

Among other decisions related to the General Plan Update, the City Council will consider
and decide on and between: (1) growth management policy options aimed at controlling non-
residential development; (2) housing policy options aimed at controlling non-residential
development; and (3) height limit policy options aimed at preserving the City's 50-foot height limit
or allowing flexibility as an incentive for good design and/or multifamily housing.

Based on the facts presented, these decisions solely concern the General Plan Update.
(Regulation 18702.2(c)(2).) Therefore, if the decisions meet the criteria set forth in Regulation
18702.2(c)(2)(A) to (D), the General Plan Exception applies.

Subparagraph (A) is satisfied because the decisions at issue only identify planning
objectives or otherwise exclusively relate to policy. The decisions were not initiated by the Public
Officials, any financial interest of the Public Officials, or by any person representing any financial
interest of the Public Officials.

Subparagraph (B) is satisfied because the decisions will require further decisions by the City
before implementing the planning or policy. The planning and policy options under consideration
with respect to the City's growth management policies, housing policies regarding non-residential
development, and height limit policies will require further decisions in order to implement their
objectives.

With respect to Subparagraph (C), there is an issue as to whether the decisions "concern an
identifiable parcel or parcels or a development project." In the Norton Advice Letter, No. I-09-058,
we determined that the General Plan Exception applied to a general plan amendment where the
decision at issue "identifies geographical regions of interest but addresses goals and objectives not
tied to specific parcels or projects." (See also the Stump Advice Letter, No. A-14-119.) You state
that none of the policy options at issue pertains to a specific lot or lots, or would consider or
approve any particular development proposal. Although certain Stanford lands may be exempted
from the application of particular policy options under consideration, those potential exemptions
address goals or objectives not tied to specific parcels or projects. Therefore, like in the Norton
Advice Letter, supra, the General Plan Update decisions at issue here, relating to the City's growth
management, housing, and height limit policies, satisfy Subparagraph (C).

Finally, Subparagraph (D) is satisfied because the General Plan Update decisions do not
concern the City's prior, concurrent, or subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, license,
zoning designation, zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent. As noted
above, you state that the policy options at issue would not consider or approve any particular
development proposal.

Therefore, the General Plan Exception applies to the General Plan Update decisions at issue
because the decisions meet the criteria of Regulation 18702.2(c)(2). Accordingly, we conclude that
the Act does not prohibit the Public Officials from taking part in those decisions.

File No. A-17-014
Page No. 8

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

Yes. The General Plan Exception applies to the General Plan Update decisions at issue

tied to specific parcels or projects." (See also the Slllmp Advice Letter, No. A-14-1 19.) You state 
that none of the policy options at issue pertains to a specific lot or lots, or would consider or 
approve any particular development proposal. Although certain Stanford lands may be exempted 
from the application of particular policy options under consideration, those potential exemptions 
address goals and objectives not tied to specific parcels or projects. Therefore, like in the Norton 
Advice Letter, supra, the General Plan Update decisions at issue here, relating to the City's growth 
management, housing, and height limit policies, satisfy Subparagraph (C). 
Finally, Subparagraph (D) is satisfied because the General Plan Update decisions do not 
concern the City's prior, concurrent, or subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, license, 
zoning designation, zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent. As noted 
above, you state that the policy options at issue would not consider or approve any particular 
development proposal. 
Therefore, the General Plan Exception applies to the General Plan Update decisions at issue 
because the decisions meet the criteria of Regulation 18702.2(c)(2). Accordingly, we conclude that 
the Act does not prohibit the Public Officials from taking part in those decisions.

QUESTION: Does the General Plan Exception set forth in Regulation l8702.2(c)(2) permit the Public 
Officials to take part in governmental decisions relating to the City's General Plan Update, 
including, but not limited to, decisions regarding: 
• Growth management policy options aimed at controlling non-residential 
development. 
• Housing policy options aimed at controlling non-residential development. 
• Height limit policy options aimed at preserving the City's 50-foot height limit 
or allowing flexibility as an incentive for good design and/or multifamily 
housing. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.

CONCLUSION: Yes. The General Plan Exception applies to the General Plan Update decisions at issue 
because the decisions meet the criteria of Regulation 18702.2(c)(2). Accordingly, we conclude that 
the Act does not prohibit the Public Officials from taking part in those decisions.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 January 30, 2017 Molly S. Stump City Attorney 250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor Palo Alto, CA 94301 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-014 Dear Ms. Stump: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmembers Tom Dubois, Eric Filseth, Liz Kniss, Lydia Kou, Greg Scharff, and Greg Tanaka, and City Manager James Keene (collectively the "Public Officials") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. This letter is based only on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTION Does the General Plan Exception set forth in Regulation 18702.2(c)(2) permit the Public Officials to take part in governmental decisions relating to the City's General Plan Update, including, but not limited to, decisions regarding: • Growth management policy options aimed at controlling non-residential development. • Housing policy options aimed at controlling non-residential development. • Height limit policy options aimed at preserving the City's 50-foot height limit or allowing flexibility as an incentive for good design and/or multifamily housing. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-014 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Yes. The General Plan Exception applies to the General Plan Update decisions at issue because the decisions meet the criteria of Regulation 18702.2(c)(2). Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Public Officials from taking part in those decisions. FACTS The City has been working on an update to its General Plan for many years. A citizens' advisory committee has recently provided its recommendations for the General Plan Update to the City Council for its consideration. As a result, the City Council will soon review the committee's work and offer direction to staff regarding key issues that must be decided before the City Council can consider the General Plan Update's adoption. Among other decisions related to the General Plan Update, the City Council will consider and decide on and between: (1) growth management policy options aimed at controlling non- residential development; (2) housing policy options aimed at controlling non-residential development; and (3) height limit policy options aimed at preserving the City's 50-foot height limit or allowing flexibility as an incentive for good design and/or
